section.title: A Cry Against Lying Lips
section.sectionRationale: This is the first of the fifteen Songs of Ascents, Psalms 120 to 134, sung by pilgrims going up to Jerusalem for the great feasts, placed in Book V of the Psalter, Psalms 107 to 150. It is an individual lament, the opening cry of the pilgrim road, voiced from a place of slander and hostility farthest from Zion.
section.thesis: Surrounded by lying lips and people who hate peace, the psalmist cries to the LORD who answers, and even while he longs only for peace, he trusts God to deal with deceit rather than answering war with war.
section.frameNote: Two movements: the cry for deliverance from a deceitful tongue and its certain judgment, verses 1 to 4, and the lament of dwelling among those who want war, verses 5 to 7.

units.label: In my distress I cried to the LORD

verses.text: In my {{tsarah|distress}} I cried to the LORD, and He answered me.
Deliver my soul, O LORD, from lying lips and a deceitful tongue.
What will He do to you, and what will be added to you, O deceitful tongue?
Sharp arrows will come from the warrior, with burning coals of the broom tree!

units.label: Too long have I dwelt among war
units.note: the ache of exile among the hostile

verses.text: Woe to me that I dwell in Meshech, that I live among the tents of Kedar!
Too long have I dwelt among those who hate {{shalom|peace}}.
I am in favor of peace; but when I speak, they want war.

tsarah.gloss: Distress, a tight or cramped place. The psalmist's trouble is pictured as confinement, and his cry is the instinct of the pressed soul turning to the LORD first.

shalom.gloss: Peace, wholeness, well-being, not merely the absence of conflict. The psalmist longs for shalom even while living among those who hate it and want war instead.

basic.context: This psalm opens the fifteen Songs of Ascents, sung by pilgrims climbing toward Jerusalem for the three annual feasts. It begins the journey from the farthest point, a place of slander and hostility, crying out to the LORD before the pilgrim road even begins to climb.
basic.hermeneutics: It describes a real experience of being lied about and surrounded by people who want war, and it prescribes the abiding pattern of crying to the LORD first and continuing to seek peace rather than repaying evil with evil.
basic.originalLanguages: The psalmist's trouble is tsarah, a tight, cramped distress, and his cry is answered. He closes longing for shalom, peace and wholeness, even while those around him hate it and choose war.
basic.typology: The psalmist's plea against lying lips anticipates Christ, who endured false witnesses at His trial and did not revile in return, entrusting Himself to the One who judges justly.
basic.christ: Jesus is the greater pilgrim who set His face to go to Jerusalem though it meant false accusation and the cross, and He Himself is our peace, breaking down the hostility this psalm laments.
basic.god: God is the one who answers the distressed cry, and He is trusted to deal justly with deceitful and warlike tongues in His own time and way.
basic.crossReferences: 1 Peter 2:23 shows Christ entrusting Himself to the just Judge rather than reviling in return. Matthew 26:59 to 60 records false witnesses seeking Jesus' destruction. Ephesians 2:14 names Christ Himself as our peace. James 3:5 to 6 describes the tongue's destructive fire.
basic.oneStory: This opening Song of Ascents cries out from the far country of deceit and hostility, the first step of a pilgrim road that climbs, psalm by psalm, toward the peace of Zion fulfilled in Christ.

context.literary: An individual lament, first of the Songs of Ascents, Psalms 120 to 134, beginning the pilgrim collection from the point farthest from Jerusalem and from God's dwelling.
context.historical: Meshech and Kedar name distant and famously hostile peoples, one to the north and one to the south, likely paired as a figure for exile among those who hate peace rather than a single itinerary.
context.canonical: Opens Book V's great pilgrim collection. Its cry answered by the LORD sets up the confident help promised immediately in the next psalm, Psalm 121.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To voice a believer's cry for deliverance from slander and hostility, and to commit the outcome to the LORD rather than to retaliation in kind.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes a specific experience of persecution by lying and warlike people. It prescribes crying out to the LORD first and continuing to pursue peace even among those bent on conflict.

debates.question: Are Meshech and Kedar literal locations or figures for hostile exile?

views.label: Literal distant peoples
views.case: Meshech, in Asia Minor, and Kedar, an Arabian tribe, are real and famously hostile peoples, so the psalmist may genuinely have sojourned among them.

views.label: Figurative language for hostility
views.case: Since the two lie at opposite ends of the map, north and south, they are more likely paired as a figure of speech for any place of exile among those who hate peace.

debates.bearing: Either way, the psalm voices the ache of living, literally or figuratively, far from God's peace among people bent on conflict.

study.originalLanguages: The psalmist's trouble is tsarah, a tight and cramped distress, met by a cry the LORD answers. His longing is for shalom, peace and wholeness, even while he dwells among those who hate it and choose war.

typology.type: The psalmist's endurance of lying lips
typology.fulfillment: Christ endured false witnesses at His trial and did not revile in return, entrusting Himself to God who judges justly, 1 Peter 2:23.

typology.type: Longing for peace amid those who want war
typology.fulfillment: Christ Himself is our peace, breaking down the dividing wall of hostility, Ephesians 2:14.

study.christ: Jesus is the greater pilgrim who set His face to go to Jerusalem, Luke 9:51, though it meant false accusation, lying witnesses, and the cross. He endured slander without reviling in return, 1 Peter 2:23, and He Himself is our peace, Ephesians 2:14, the true answer to this psalm's longing among those who hate peace.
study.god: God is the one who answers the cry of the distressed, and He is trusted to deal justly with deceitful and warlike tongues rather than leaving His people to avenge themselves.

crossReferences.note: Christ, reviled, did not revile in return but entrusted Himself to Him who judges justly, as the psalmist entrusts his cause to the LORD.
Jesus endured false witnesses seeking His destruction, echoing the psalmist's plea for deliverance from lying lips.
Christ Himself is our peace, the answer to the psalmist's longing among those who hate peace.
The tongue's destructive power, likened to fire, echoes the psalm's image of burning coals against the deceitful tongue.

study.oneStory: This opening Song of Ascents cries out from the far country of deceit and hostility, the first step of a pilgrim road that climbs, psalm by psalm, toward the peace of Zion fulfilled in Christ.

sermon.title: A Pilgrim's Journey
sermon.why: Begg works through Psalms 120 to 122 together as the launch of the pilgrim's ascent, showing how the cry against lying lips and the longing for peace begins the journey toward the LORD's help and presence in Zion.

quiz.q: What does the psalmist ask the LORD to deliver him from?

options.text: Lying lips and a deceitful tongue.
options.note: Verse 2 names the specific trouble: slander and deceit.

options.text: A physical illness.
options.note: The psalm names deceitful speech, not sickness, as the trouble.

options.text: Financial poverty.
options.note: The distress is relational and verbal, not financial.

options.text: A coming famine.
options.note: Close in seriousness, but the text specifically names lying lips, not famine.

quiz.q: What image pictures the judgment coming on the deceitful tongue?

options.text: A warrior's sharp arrows and burning coals of the broom tree.
options.note: Verse 4 gives this vivid image of sudden and lasting judgment.

options.text: A gentle rebuke from a friend.
options.note: The image is severe and warlike, not a mild correction.

options.text: A slow fading like grass.
options.note: That image appears elsewhere, in Psalm 129, not here.

options.text: A flood sweeping the land.
options.note: Flood imagery appears in Psalm 124; this psalm uses arrows and coals.

quiz.q: What do Meshech and Kedar represent in this psalm?

options.text: Dwelling among hostile, war loving peoples, far from peace.
options.note: These distant, opposite regions picture the ache of exile among those who hate peace.

options.text: Wealthy trading partners of Israel.
options.note: The emphasis is on hostility and distance from peace, not commerce.

options.text: Close allies who kept covenant with Israel.
options.note: The text laments dwelling among them precisely because they are hostile, not allied.

options.text: Cities within the promised land.
options.note: Both regions lie outside the land, underscoring the psalmist's sense of exile.

quiz.q: What is the psalmist's own stance toward peace, even surrounded by hostility?

options.text: He is for peace, but when he speaks, others want war.
options.note: Verse 7 states this tension plainly.

options.text: He also wants war, matching his enemies.
options.note: The text explicitly says he favors peace, not war.

options.text: He is indifferent to peace or war.
options.note: He actively declares himself for peace, not neutral.

options.text: He seeks peace only after winning by force.
options.note: The psalm shows him desiring peace now, not after conquest.

quiz.q: How does this psalm's cry against lying lips connect to Christ?

options.text: Christ endured false witnesses and did not revile in return, entrusting Himself to God's just judgment.
options.note: 1 Peter 2:23 shows Jesus embodying this pattern at His trial and on the cross.

options.text: It has no connection to the New Testament.
options.note: The pattern of enduring slander while trusting God's justice is directly fulfilled in Christ.

options.text: It predicts the exact words spoken against Jesus.
options.note: The connection is one of pattern and fulfillment, not verbatim prediction.

options.text: It shows Christ retaliating against His accusers.
options.note: Christ notably did not revile in return, the opposite of retaliation.

questions: The psalmist's first instinct in distress is to cry out to the LORD. When trouble comes, is your first move to speak to God, or do you turn there only after trying everything else?
The psalm names a specific pain: lying lips and a deceitful tongue. Where have you been wounded by others' words, and how does it help to know God hears that cry?
The psalmist longs for peace even while living among those who want war. Where are you tempted to answer hostility with hostility rather than continuing to seek peace?

Christ endured false witnesses without reviling in return, entrusting Himself to God who judges justly. What would it look like this week to leave a wrong done to you in God's hands rather than your own?

This is the first step of a long pilgrim road toward Zion. What distant, weary place are you crying out from today, and how does it help to know this is only the beginning of the ascent toward God's presence?

translationNotes.note: The broom tree's coals were known for burning unusually hot and long, making them a fitting image of judgment that is both sudden and lasting.

Meshech and Kedar lie at opposite ends of the ancient world, one north and one south, so the pairing likely functions as a figure of speech for hostile exile in general rather than naming one specific journey.
The phrase rendered I am in favor of peace can also be read I am peace itself, intensifying the contrast with those around him who want war.

meta.notes: BSB text taken verbatim from biblehub.com's BSB page for Psalm 120 (the local makor-scripture toolkit returned a disk I/O error, so the web fallback was used for all of Psalms 120-132). Verse count (7) and wording cross-checked against the section map bounds Psalms 120:1-7. No em or en dashes present. Sermon link verified this session via WebSearch and web_fetch confirming Alistair Begg's sermon 'A Pilgrim's Journey' (covering Psalms 120-122) at truthforlife.org.
